When the parabola is y² = 4ax, the student reads a = 1 regardless of the coefficient (e.g., for y² = 12x, using a = 1 instead of a = 3), leading to wrong focus, directrix, and all derived quantities.

When finding common tangents to a parabola and another curve, the student finds only tangent lines with positive slope and misses negative-slope solutions (or vice versa), forgetting that tangency conditions can yield multiple valid slopes.

When reflecting a parabola across a line, the student reflects individual points correctly but fails to substitute back to get the reflected curve equation, or uses the reflection formula for the wrong line (e.g., reflecting across y = x using the x-axis formula).
The student writes the normal equation when the tangent is needed or vice versa, confusing the slope of the tangent (1/t) with the slope of the normal (−t) at a parametric point.

The student correctly identifies that the closest point on the parabola lies along the normal but solves for the wrong root of the cubic normal equation, selecting a point that gives a local maximum or saddle point instead of the minimum.
When the parabola is y² = 4ax, the student reads a = 1 regardless of the coefficient (e.g., for y² = 12x, using a = 1 instead of a = 3), leading to wrong focus, directrix, and all derived quantities.

error_name: Sign Error in Focal Chord Relation
description: The student uses t₁t₂ = 1 instead of t₁t₂ = −1 for focal chord endpoints, or confuses the sign convention when one endpoint has a negative parameter.

When finding common tangents to a parabola and another curve, the student finds only tangent lines with positive slope and misses negative-slope solutions (or vice versa), forgetting that tangency conditions can yield multiple valid slopes.
The student writes the normal equation when the tangent is needed or vice versa, confusing the slope of the tangent (1/t) with the slope of the normal (−t) at a parametric point.

error_name: Area Sign Neglect for Oriented Triangles
description: When computing the area of a triangle whose vertices lie on the parabola using the determinant formula, the student neglects absolute value and reports a negative area or misidentifies the orientation.

error_name: Optimizing distance instead of squared distance
description: In shortest-distance problems, the student minimizes the distance function directly instead of the squared distance, leading to unnecessary square root differentiation and algebraic errors.

error_name: Missing a root of f' due to incomplete factorization
description: The student factors f'(x) partially and misses a critical point — especially when the derivative factors into a product with both polynomial and trigonometric/exponential factors.

error_name: Dropping the constraint in geometric optimization
description: In wire-cutting or inscribed-figure problems, the student writes the objective function in two variables but fails to properly substitute the constraint to reduce to one variable.

error_name: Sign error in chain rule through composition
description: When analyzing g(x) = f(h(x)), the student forgets that g'(x) = f'(h(x))·h'(x) and ignores the sign of h'(x), incorrectly concluding monotonicity from f' alone.

error_name: Confusing local and absolute extrema on closed intervals
description: The student finds only interior critical points and neglects endpoint evaluation when asked for absolute max/min on a closed interval.

error_name: Incorrect implicit differentiation with mixed terms
description: When differentiating equations like x²y² − 2x = 4(1 − y), the student applies the product rule incorrectly to the x²y² term or forgets dy/dx on y-containing terms.

When a circle touches a coordinate axis, the student fails to set the correct relationship (e.g., for touching the x-axis: |k| = r, or for touching y-axis: |h| = r), or confuses which coordinate of the centre equals the radius.

The student finds all algebraic solutions but fails to reject solutions that violate stated geometric constraints such as 'lies in the first quadrant', 'closer to the origin', 'circle lies below x-axis', resulting in extraneous answers.

The student extracts centre as (g, f) instead of (−g, −f) from the general form x² + y² + 2gx + 2fy + c = 0, propagating the wrong centre through all subsequent calculations.

When computing the common chord of two circles by subtracting their equations, the student subtracts in the wrong order or makes algebraic errors in simplification, yielding an incorrect radical axis equation.

When the problem involves a chord as diameter of another circle or angle in semicircle = 90°, the student fails to recognize this configuration and sets up an unnecessarily complicated system instead of directly using the diametric form (x−x₁)(x−x₂) + (y−y₁)(y−y₂) = 0.

error_name: Confusing Tangent Length with Distance to Centre
description: The student computes the distance from an external point to the centre of the circle and reports it as the tangent length, instead of using √(S₁) = √(d² − r²).

error_name: Treating Limit as Derivative Without Verification
description: The student recognizes a limit resembles a derivative definition but misidentifies which function's derivative it represents, or incorrectly handles the algebraic rearrangement needed to match the standard definition form.
The student verifies continuity at a junction point and declares the function differentiable, forgetting that matching left and right derivatives is a separate and additional requirement.

The student computes only one one-sided limit (typically the right-hand limit) and assumes the function is continuous/differentiable without checking that the left-hand limit matches, especially near absolute value or floor function boundaries.
The student miscounts where [f(x)] is discontinuous by missing values of x where f(x) passes through an integer, especially when f is non-monotonic (e.g., quadratic under a floor).

error_name: Treating Limit as Derivative Without Verification
description: The student recognizes a limit resembles a derivative definition but misidentifies which function's derivative it represents, or incorrectly handles the algebraic rearrangement needed to match the standard definition form.

The student expands sinx, cosx, eˣ, or log(1+x) to too few terms, causing premature cancellation that yields 0/0 again instead of the actual limit; the student either gives up or incorrectly concludes the limit is 0 or ∞.

The student applies L'Hôpital's rule without verifying that the form is actually 0/0 or ∞/∞, or applies it when simpler algebraic manipulation (factoring, standard limits) would be more efficient and less error-prone.

The student expands sinx, cosx, eˣ, or log(1+x) to too few terms, causing premature cancellation that yields 0/0 again instead of the actual limit; the student either gives up or incorrectly concludes the limit is 0 or ∞.

error_name: Absolute Value Sign Error at Zero Crossings
description: When analyzing |f(x)|, the student either misidentifies the zeros of f(x) or incorrectly assigns the sign of f(x) in sub-intervals, leading to wrong derivative expressions on one side.
When analyzing f∘g, the student evaluates g at a point but then applies the wrong piece of f because they fail to check which piece of f corresponds to the output value of g.

In expressions like x·sin²x or x·tan²x in the denominator, the student replaces sin²x with x² immediately but forgets that the remaining cos or higher-order correction terms in the numerator still matter at the required order of cancellation.

When asked for distance from a point to a plane 'measured along' or 'parallel to' a given line, student computes the standard perpendicular distance using the point-to-plane formula instead of parametrizing the line through the point and finding its intersection with the plane.

Student computes the cross product of direction vectors for shortest distance but reverses the order of the vectors, getting a sign error in the numerator that yields the wrong magnitude when taking the absolute value is missed or the scalar triple product sign matters for a subsequent step.

When a line is given as the intersection of two planes (e.g., 3y − 2z − 1 = 0 = 3x − z + 4), student attempts to use it in symmetric form formulas without first converting it, or converts incorrectly by solving the system incompletely.

Student sets up the scalar triple product determinant for coplanarity of two lines but uses incorrect rows — e.g., using position vectors of points instead of the connecting vector between points on the two lines.

When finding a plane through the intersection of two planes, student writes P₁ + λP₂ = 0 but then applies the constraint to find λ and makes an arithmetic error in substitution because the parametric form involves sums of coefficients that grow unwieldy.

When asked for distance from a point to a plane 'measured along' or 'parallel to' a given line, student computes the standard perpendicular distance using the point-to-plane formula instead of parametrizing the line through the point and finding its intersection with the plane.

Student uses the dot-product-equals-zero condition when the question requires parallel lines (proportional direction ratios), or conversely uses proportionality when perpendicularity is needed, especially in questions asking for conditions on parameters.

When finding the mirror image, student computes the foot of perpendicular correctly but then reports the foot as the image instead of using the midpoint formula (image = 2×foot − original point) to find the actual image.

error_name: Confusing Tangent Length with Distance to Centre
description: The student computes the distance from an external point to the centre of the circle and reports it as the tangent length, instead of using √(S₁) = √(d² − r²).

error_name: Chord of Contact vs Tangent at a Point Confusion
description: The student writes the equation of the tangent at a point on the circle when the problem asks for the chord of contact from an external point (or vice versa), using T = 0 incorrectly in context.

error_name: Missing a root of f' due to incomplete factorization
description: The student factors f'(x) partially and misses a critical point — especially when the derivative factors into a product with both polynomial and trigonometric/exponential factors.

error_name: Confusing local and absolute extrema on closed intervals
description: The student finds only interior critical points and neglects endpoint evaluation when asked for absolute max/min on a closed interval.

error_name: Confusing local and absolute extrema on closed intervals
description: The student finds only interior critical points and neglects endpoint evaluation when asked for absolute max/min on a closed interval.

error_name: Confusing f'(x) = 0 with f'' > 0 for convexity argument
description: When given f'' > 0 abstractly and asked about g(x) = f(x) + f(1-x), the student tries to compute explicit derivatives instead of reasoning from convexity and the increasing nature of f'.

error_name: Sign error in chain rule through composition
description: When analyzing g(x) = f(h(x)), the student forgets that g'(x) = f'(h(x))·h'(x) and ignores the sign of h'(x), incorrectly concluding monotonicity from f' alone.

error_name: Ignoring critical points where f' is undefined
description: The student only solves f'(x) = 0 and misses extrema at points where the derivative does not exist, such as cusps from absolute values or fractional exponents (e.g., x^(2/3) terms).

description: When differentiating equations like x²y² − 2x = 4(1 − y), the student applies the product rule incorrectly to the x²y² term or forgets dy/dx on y-containing terms.

error_name: Rolle's theorem applied without checking equal endpoint values
description: The student applies Rolle's theorem without verifying f(a) = f(b), or confuses it with LMVT, leading to incorrect identification of the guaranteed point c.

error_name: Missing a root of f' due to incomplete factorization
description: The student factors f'(x) partially and misses a critical point — especially when the derivative factors into a product with both polynomial and trigonometric/exponential factors.
The student misapplies divisibility rules (e.g., using the digit-sum rule for divisibility by 4, which actually depends on the last two digits, not the digit sum).
The student treats repeated letters (e.g., the two S's in ASSASSINATION) as distinct, failing to divide by the factorial of the repeat count, inflating the answer.

description: The student factors f'(x) partially and misses a critical point — especially when the derivative factors into a product with both polynomial and trigonometric/exponential factors.
